questions.question: The first compound to be prepared in the laboratory was:
questions.question_raw: The first compound to be prepared in the laboratory was:
questions.explanation: The first compound to be prepared in the laboratory was urea (also known as carbamide). It is an organic compound with the chemical formula NH2CONH2 (two NH2 (amine) groups joined by a carbonyl group).

questions.question: Hybridisation in methane (CH4) ethene (C2H4) ethyne (C2H2) involves s and p orbitals. Choose the correct hybrid orbitals in the options given below for CH4, C2H4 and C2H2 respectively:
questions.question_raw: Hybridisation in methane (CH4) ethene (C2H4) ethyne (C2H2) involves s and p orbitals. Choose the correct hybrid orbitals in the options given below for CH4, C2H4 and C2H2 respectively:
questions.explanation: In methane C is sp3 hybridised because of 3 sigma bond formation. While in ethane both the C are sp2 hybridised, and in ethyne both the C are sp hybridised.

questions.question: Change in hybridisation affects the carbon’s and the organic compound’s:
questions.question_raw: Change in hybridisation affects the carbon’s and the organic compound’s:
questions.explanation:
In hybridization as % of 's' character increases electronegativity also increases.
More the s character more will be the electronegativity of that atom.
Increasing order of electronegativity with %s character is sp (50%) > sp2(33.33%) > sp3(25%).

questions.question: 2-bromo butane can be represented in various ways. Choose the incorrect one from the following
questions.question_raw: 2-bromo butane can be represented in various ways. Choose the incorrect one from the following
questions.explanation: methyl group is represented at 3rd carbon no bromine is there on 2nd carbon

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name?
questions.explanation: While writing IUPAC name, alkyl groups are written in alphabetical order. thus lower locant 3 is assigned to ethyl. prefix di, tri or tetra are not included in alphabetical order

questions.question: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in H2C = O and mention the molecular geometry also.
questions.question_raw: Write the state of hybridisation of carbon in H2C = O and mention the molecular geometry also.
questions.explanation: Due to the presence of a double bond in the carbonyl carbon ( {tex}>\mathrm C=\mathrm O{/tex}), carbon is sp2 hybridised. This hybridization leads to a trigonal planar geometry which means a bond angle of 120° around the C.

questions.question: In $\pi$ (pi) bond formation, one of the below criteria is necessary. Choose the appropriate one.
questions.question_raw: In {tex}\pi{/tex} (pi) bond formation, one of the below criteria is necessary. Choose the appropriate one.

options.text: trigonal orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.
planar orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.
parallel orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.
pentagonal orientation of the two p orbitals on adjacent atoms.

questions.explanation: Parallel orientation of p orbitals on adjacent atoms is necessary for side-ways overlapping and formation of {tex}\pi{/tex} bond.

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.question_raw: Delocalisation of σ-electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p-orbital is called
questions.explanation: The interaction of the electrons in a sigma bond (usually C–H or C–C) with an adjacent empty (or partially filled) non-bonding p-orbital, antibonding {tex}\sigma{/tex} or {tex}\pi{/tex} orbital or filled {tex}\pi{/tex} orbital, to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system is Hyperconjugation.

questions.question: Hyperconjugation in an organic compound is a
questions.question_raw: Hyperconjugation in an organic compound is a

options.text: delocalisation of σ electrons of C -C bond of an alkyl group

questions.explanation: Hyperconjugation is the stabilizing interaction that results from the interaction of the electrons in a C-H {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond with an adjacent empty p orbital or a {tex}\pi{/tex} orbital to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system. It is a permanent effect.

questions.question: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.question_raw: Covalent bonds are formed with other carbon atoms due to:
questions.explanation: Catenation is the linkage of atoms of the same element into longer chains. Catenation occurs most readily in carbon, which forms covalent bonds with other carbon atoms to form longer chains and structures. This is the reason for the presence of the vast number of organic compounds in nature.

questions.question: Which of the following organic compound Wohler synthesised from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate in the presence of heat?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following organic compound Wohler synthesised from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate in the presence of heat?
questions.explanation: The reaction can be given as starting with solutions of potassium cyanate and ammonium chloride which are mixed, heated and cooled again to get ammonium cyanate salt which on decomposition gives the urea.

Straight or branched chain compounds, for example, the below ones are called
{tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits^{\text{|}} }\limits^{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{{\text{||}}} }\limits^{\text{O}} {\text{ - H}}{/tex}
questions.explanation: The straight and branched chain compounds are known as aliphatic compounds (also called acyclic or open chain compounds).

questions.question: Correct IUPAC name for ${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}\,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}$ is ________.
questions.question_raw: Correct IUPAC name for {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}\,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,\mathop {\mathop {{\text{CH}}}\limits_{\text{|}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{/tex} is ________.
questions.explanation: 3, 4 - Dimethylhexane will be correct IUPAC name as 6 membered carbon chain will be the parent chain with methyl groups at positions 3 and 4.

questions.question: The molecular formula C3H8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.question_raw: The molecular formula C3H8O represents two alcohols: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol. This property is called as:
questions.explanation: propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol are positional isomers as they differ only in the position of functional group -OH. Positional isomers are isomers that have the same carbon skeleton and the same functional groups but differ from each other in the location of the functional groups on or in the carbon chain.

questions.question: Propanal and propanone are
questions.question_raw: Propanal and propanone are
questions.explanation: Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism. For example, the molecular formula C3H6O represents both a ketone and aldehyde:{tex}CH_3COCH_3\; and\;CH_3CH_2CHO{/tex}

questions.question: If an –NH2 functional group is present in an organic compound it is called amine. If a -CONH2 functional group is present in an organic compound then it is called:
questions.question_raw: If an –NH2 functional group is present in an organic compound it is called amine. If a -CONH2 functional group is present in an organic compound then it is called:
questions.explanation: -CONH2 is amide functional group. Amides are formed by conversion of a carboxylic acid into ammonium salt and then heating it.

questions.question: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.question_raw: A covalent bond when cleaved heterolytically yields:
questions.explanation: Heterolytic cleavage produces carbonium ion and carbanion. In heterolytic cleavage, the bond breaks in such a fashion that the shared pair of electrons remains with one of the fragments. After heterolysis, one atom has a sextet electronic structure and a positive charge and the other, a valence octet with at least one lone pair and a negative charge. A species having a carbon atom possessing electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation (earlier called carbonium ion). There is also a carbon species carrying a negative charge on a carbon atom which gets the unshared pair of electrons which is called carbanion.

questions.question: In which of the following, functional group isomerism is not possible?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following, functional group isomerism is not possible?
questions.explanation: Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism. Alkyl halides do not show functional isomerism.

questions.question: In which of the following compounds the carbon marked with asterisk is expected to have greatest partial positive charge?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following compounds the carbon marked with asterisk is expected to have greatest partial positive charge?
questions.explanation: Cl is most electronegative amongst Cl, Br and I and has more -I (inductive electron withdrawing) effect. So it causes the asterisk marked to have the maximum partial positive charge.

questions.question: C4H10O represents methoxypropane CH3OC3H7 and ethoxyethane (C2H5OC2H5):
questions.question_raw: C4H10O represents methoxypropane CH3OC3H7 and ethoxyethane (C2H5OC2H5):
questions.explanation: Metamerism arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule. For example, C4H10O represents methoxypropane (CH3OC3H7) and ethoxyethane (C2H5OC2H5).

questions.explanation: The aliphatic heterocyclic compounds are the cyclic analogues of amines, ethers, thioethers and their properties are influenced by the ring strain.

questions.question: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:
questions.question_raw: The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups in the naming of an organic compound is:

options.text: - CONH2, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH2, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl
- COCl, - CONH2, - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH2, - COOR (R = alkyl group)
- COOR (R = alkyl group right), - COCl, - CONH2 , - CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - H2
- CN, - HC = O, > C = O, - OH, - NH2, - COOR (R = alkyl group), - COCl, - CONH2

questions.explanation:
COOR (R=alkyl group) > -COCl,> -CONH2 > -CN > -HC=O>  >C=O> -OH, -NH2 is the decreasing order of priority
ester > acyl halide > acyl amide > nitrile > aldehyde > ketones > alcohol > amine

questions.question: The following compounds are called:
questions.question_raw: The following compounds are called:
questions.explanation: Aromatic compounds may also have heteroatom in the ring. Such compounds are called heterocyclic aromatic compounds. Thus the given compounds are aromatic heterocyclic compounds namely furan, thiophene and pyridine respectively.

questions.question: In which bond of CH3CH2CH2Br the inductive effect is expected to be the least?
questions.question_raw: In which bond of CH3CH2CH2Br the inductive effect is expected to be the least?
questions.explanation: As the distance of the functional group increases its inductive effect decreases. Carbon-3 is farthest from Br. So in the bond between carbon-2 and carbon-3 the inductive effect is the least.

questions.question: Sodium cyanide, sulphide, and halide, (-CN, -S, and -H, coming from the organic compound) formed on fusion with sodium are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This is called:
questions.question_raw: Sodium cyanide, sulphide, and halide, (-CN, -S, and -H, coming from the organic compound) formed on fusion with sodium are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This is called:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus present in an organic compound are detected by “Lassaigne’s test”. The elements present in the compound are converted from the covalent form into the ionic form by fusing the compound with sodium metal. Cyanide, sulphide, and halide of sodium so formed on sodium fusion are extracted from the fused mass by boiling it with distilled water. This extract is known as sodium fusion extract.

questions.question: Geometrical isomers and Optical isomers are:
questions.question_raw: Geometrical isomers and Optical isomers are:
questions.explanation: They are stereoisomers as they differ in the orientation of atoms in space.

questions.question:
Acid catalysed electrophilic addition reaction to carbon-carbon double bond proceeds in two steps. The first step involves the addition of an H+ to double bond. Name the type of intermediate formed in the first step of the following addition reaction:
${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}-{\text{HC }} = {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}^ + } \to ?$
questions.question_raw:
Acid catalysed electrophilic addition reaction to carbon-carbon double bond proceeds in two steps. The first step involves the addition of an H+ to double bond. Name the type of intermediate formed in the first step of the following addition reaction:
{tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}-{\text{HC }} = {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}^ + } \to ?{/tex}
questions.explanation: In this reaction, formation of secondary carbocation takes place by the addition of H+ to terminal carbon of carbon-carbon double bond among the two possible carbon atoms. The more stable secondary carbocation formation will take place instead of primary carbocation.

questions.question: Polarisation of $\sigma$-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent $\sigma$-bond is termed as:
questions.question_raw: Polarisation of {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is termed as:
questions.explanation: Polarisation of {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond caused by the polarisation of the adjacent {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is referred to as the inductive effect. This effect is passed on to the subsequent bonds also but the effect decreases rapidly as the number of intervening bonds increases and becomes vanishingly small after three bonds. The inductive effect is related to the ability of substituent(s) to either withdraw or donate electron density to the attached carbon atom.

questions.question: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is called:
questions.question_raw: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is called:
questions.explanation: Delocalisation of σ electrons of C - H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital is known as Hyperconjugation. It is a general stabilising interaction. The {tex}\sigma{/tex} electrons of C- H bond of the alkyl group enter into partial conjugation with the attached unsaturated system or with the unshared p orbital. It is a permanent effect.

questions.question: Which one of the following compounds is used in the estimation of oxygen?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following compounds is used in the estimation of oxygen?
questions.explanation: I2O5 is used. A definite mass of an organic compound is decomposed by heating in a stream of nitrogen gas. The mixture of gaseous products containing oxygen is passed over red-hot coke when all the oxygen is converted to carbon monoxide. This mixture is passed through warm iodine pentoxide when carbon monoxide is oxidised to carbon dioxide producing iodine.

questions.question: In Carius method, the organic compound is heated with
questions.question_raw: In Carius method, the organic compound is heated with
questions.explanation: In carius method of estimation of halogens, aknown mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a hard glass tube known as Carius tube, in a furnace.

questions.question: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.question_raw: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
1 M of 10 mL H2SO4= 1 M of 20 mL NH3
1000 mL of 1 M ammonia contains 14 g nitrogen
20 mL of 1 M ammonia contains {tex}\frac{{(14 \times 20g)}}{{1000}}{/tex} nitrogen
% of nitrogen = {tex} \frac{{(14 \times 20 \times 100)}}{{1000 \times 0.5}}{/tex} = 56.0%

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name of the given compound?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name of the given compound?
questions.explanation: In IUPAC name, functional groups are written in alphabetical order. Locant 3 is assigned to ethyl group and the two methyl groups are present on C-4. The longest C chain has 7 C atoms so the IUPAC name is 3-ethyl-4,4-dimethylheptane.

questions.question: When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions, what happens?
questions.question_raw: When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions, what happens?

options.text: inductive and electromeric effects operate with the same magnitude.

questions.explanation: Electromeric Effect (E effect) is a temporary effect. The organic compounds having multiple bonds (a double or triple bond) show this effect in the presence of an attacking reagent only. It is defined as the complete transfer of a shared pair of {tex}\pi{/tex}-electrons to one of the atoms joined by multiple bonds on the demand of an attacking reagent. Polarisation of {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond caused by the polarisation of adjacent {tex}\sigma{/tex}-bond is referred to as the inductive effect. When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions then electromeric effect predominates.

questions.question: Nucleophile is a species that should have:
questions.question_raw: Nucleophile is a species that should have:

options.text: a pair of electrons to donate

questions.explanation: A nucleophile means a nucleus loving species. It can be a neutral molecule with a pair of electron to donate or it can be a negatively charged species.

questions.question: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
questions.question_raw: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of AgBr = 108 + 80 = 188 g/mol

188 g AgBr contains 80 g bromine
0.12 g AgBr contains (80 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.12)/188 g bromine
Percentage of bromine {tex} = \frac{{(80 \times 0.12 \times 100)}}{{(188 \times 0.15)}} = 34.04\% {/tex}

questions.question: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
questions.question_raw: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of carbondioxide=44 g/mol

Molar mass of C=12 g/mol

44 g CO​​​​​2 contains 12 g C

0.198 g of CO​​​​​2 contains (12 x 0.198 g)/44 C

% of C= (12x0.198x100)/(44x0.246)=21.95%

Molar mass of water=18 g/mol

18 g water contains 2 g H

0.1014 g water contains (2x0.1014 g)/18H

% of H= (2 x 0.1014 x 100)/(0.246 x 18)=4.58 %

questions.question: Which of the following carbocation is most stable?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following carbocation is most stable?
questions.explanation: {tex}{(C{H_3})_3}\mathop C\limits^ \oplus {/tex} i.e. the tertiary carbocation is most stable. Alkyl groups directly attached to the positively charged carbon stabilise the carbocations due to inductive and hyperconjugation effects.

questions.question: Which of the following is a property of a carbocation?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is a property of a carbocation?

options.text: it has a sextet of electrons

questions.explanation: A species having a carbon atom possessing a sextet of electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation (earlier called carbonium ion). Carbocations are highly unstable and reactive species. They are formed by heterolytic cleavage.

questions.question: The bond that undergoes heterolytic cleavage most readily is:
questions.question_raw: The bond that undergoes heterolytic cleavage most readily is:
questions.explanation: Due to the electronegativity difference between O and H atom, the O-H bond gets polarized and rapidly breaks to give H+ and O2- ions.

questions.question:
The reaction
$C{H_3}CH = C{H_2} + HBr\xrightarrow{Peroxide} C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br$ can be explained by
questions.question_raw:
The reaction
{tex}C{H_3}CH = C{H_2} + HBr\xrightarrow{Peroxide} C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}Br{/tex}  can be explained by
questions.explanation: In the presence of peroxide and light, the addition of HBr to unsymmetrical alkenes occurs contrary to Markovnikov's rule. The chemistry follows a free-radical mechanism. Organic reactions, which proceed by homolytic fission are called free radical or homopolar or nonpolar reactions.

questions.question: Which of the following is an electrophile?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is an electrophile?
questions.explanation: A reagent that takes away an electron pair is called electrophile (E+) i.e., electron seeking. In neutral molecules such as alkyl halides, due to the polarity of the C-X bond a partial positive charge is generated on the carbon atom and hence the carbon atom becomes an electrophilic centre at which a nucleophile can attack. Dimethyl chloride is thus an electrophile.

questions.question: Which of the following compound will exhibit cis-trans isomerism?

questions.explanation:
2-butene has the following cis and transforms

In cis-2-butene the same groups (-CH3) lie on the same side of the double bond whereas in trans-2-butene same groups lie on the opposite side of the double bond.

questions.question: The pair that does not require calcination is
questions.question_raw: The pair that does not require calcination is
questions.explanation:
The hydroxide, hydrated oxides and carbonate ores, after concentration, are subjected to calcination. In the process, the ore is heated below its melting point in the limited supply or absence of air. As the result, these are converted into their oxides.
So, among the given options, the options having either carbonates (e.g. ZnCO3 and CaCO3 {tex}\cdot{/tex} MgCO3) or hydrated oxide (e.g. Fe2O3 {tex}\cdot{/tex} xH2O), require calcination while pair, i.e. ZnO and MgO does not require calcination.

questions.question: 25 g of an unknown hydrocarbon upon burning produces 88 g of CO2 and 9 g of H2O. This unknown hydrocarbon contains
questions.question_raw: 25 g of an unknown hydrocarbon upon burning produces 88 g of CO2 and 9 g of H2O. This unknown hydrocarbon contains

options.text: 22 g of carbon and 3 g of hydrogen
20 g of carbon and 5 g of hydrogen
18 g of carbon and 7 g of hydrogen
24 g of carbon and 1 g of hydrogen

questions.explanation:
Hydrocarbon containing C and H upon burning produces CO2 and water vapour respectively. The equation is represented as
CxHy + (x + {tex}\frac{y}{4}{/tex})O2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} xCO2 + ({tex}\frac{y}{2}{/tex})H2O
Mass of carbon = {tex}\frac{12}{44}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} mass of CO2 
{tex}\frac{12}{44}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 88g = 24g
Mass of hydrogen = {tex}\frac{12}{18}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} mass of H2O
{tex}\frac{12}{18}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 1g
So, the unknown hydrocarbon contains 24g of carbon and 1g of hydrogen.

questions.question: An organic compound A is oxidized with Na2O2 followed by boiling with HNO3. The resultant solution is then treated with ammonium molybdate to yield a yellow precipitate. Based on the above observation, the element present in the given compound is
questions.question_raw: An organic compound A is oxidized with Na2O2 followed by boiling with HNO3. The resultant solution is then treated with ammonium molybdate to yield a yellow precipitate. Based on the above observation, the element present in the given compound is
questions.explanation:
Organic compound 'A' contain phosphorus as it gives positive test with ammonium molybdate. Phosphorus present in organic compound 'A' get oxidised with Na2O2 and form Na3PO4.
{tex}\mathop {2P + {\text{ }}5N{a_2}{O_2}}\limits_{Compound} \to {\text{ }}\mathop {2N{a_3}P{O_4} + {\text{ }}2N{a_2}O}\limits_{Sodium{\text{ }}phosphate} {/tex}
Na3PO4 in presence of HNO3 form H3PO4 and NaNO3.
Na3PO4+ 3HNO3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} H3PO4 + 3NaNO3
Upon cooling, a few drops of ammonium molybdate solution are added. A yellow ppt. confirms the presence of phosphorus in the organic compound.
H3PO4 + 12(NH4)2MoO4 + 21HNO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_4}} \right)}_3}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_4} \cdot 12{\text{Mo}}{{\text{O}}_3} \downarrow }\limits_{Yellowppt.} {/tex}  + 21NH4NO3 + 12H2O

questions.question:
The increasing order of nucleophilicity of the following nucleophiles is:
 $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$
 H2O
 $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}^{-}$
 OH-
questions.question_raw:
The increasing order of nucleophilicity of the following nucleophiles is:

  1. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}{/tex}
  2. H2O
  3. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
  4. OH-
questions.explanation:
Higher the basicity of a base, stronger will be its nucleophilic character. Further a stronger base has a weaker conjugate acid. The acidic order of the conjugate acid of the given bases is
H3O+ > CH3SO3H > CH3COOH > H2O
Thus the increasing order of the basic nature or nucleophilicity of the given species is
{tex}\mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{(ii)} \, < \,\mathop {C{H_3}SO_3^ - }\limits_{(iii)} \, < \,\mathop {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }}\limits_{(i)} \, < \,\mathop {O{H^ - }}\limits_{(iv)} {/tex}

questions.question: In chromatography, which of the following statements is incorrect for Rf?
questions.question_raw: In chromatography, which of the following statements is incorrect for Rf?

options.text: Rf value is dependent on the mobile phase
Rf value depends on the type of chromatography
Higher Rf value means higher adsorption
The value of Rf can not be more than one

questions.explanation:
In chromatography, the expression of retention factor (Rf) is
Rf = {tex}\frac{{{\text{Distance travelled by the compound from origin}}}}{{{\text{Distance travelled by the solvent from origin}}}}<1{/tex}
The value of Rf  signifies the relative ratio of migration of each component of the mixture with respect to the developing solvent used. Rf value depends on the type of adsorption chromatography like TLC (Thin-Layer Chromatography), paper chromatography etc. The Rf value is also the characteristic of a compound (sample) for a given developing solvent at a given temperature.
When the compound in the sample (usually less polar) is weakly adsorbed the spot will travel a shorter distance from the origin and hence the Rf value will be decreased.

questions.question: The principle of column chromatography is
questions.question_raw: The principle of column chromatography is

options.text: differential adsorption of the substances on the solid phase
differential absorption of the substances on the solid phase

questions.explanation:
In column chromatography, separation of mixture of compounds (adsorbate) takes place over a column of solid adsorbent (silica gel and Al2O3) packed in a glass tube. When an appropriate eluant (liquid) is allowed to flow down the column, the compounds present in the mixture get adsorbed to different extent on the adsorbent column and thus complete separation takes place.
Thus, column chromatography is based on the differential adsorption of the substance on the solid phase.

questions.question:
In the following compound
the favourable site/s for protonation is/are
questions.question_raw:
In the following compound

the favourable site/s for protonation is/are
questions.explanation: All sites (a, b, c, d, e) of the given molecule have lone pair on N-atoms. Higher the ease of donation of Ip of electrons of N, more favourable will be the site for protonation. Ease of donation of Ip of electrons, i.e. Lewis basicity inversely depends on the percentage of s-character in the hybridisation of ‘N’ which will decide the electronegativity of ‘N'. At ‘a' and 'e' N-atoms are sp3 (s% = 25) hybridised, whereas at 'b', 'c' and ‘d', N-atoms are sp2 (s% = 33) hybridised. So, ‘b', ‘c’ and 'd' are the favourable sites for protonation (H+ is a Lewis acid, i.e. electrons acceptor).

questions.question: An organic compound is estimated through Dumas method and was found to evolved 6 moles of CO2, 4 moles of H2O and 1 mole of nitrogen gas. The formula of the compound is
questions.question_raw: An organic compound is estimated through Dumas method and was found to evolved 6 moles of CO2, 4 moles of H2O and 1 mole of nitrogen gas. The formula of the compound is
questions.explanation:
In the Dumas method, the organic compound is heated with dry cupric oxide in a combustion tube in the atmosphere of CO2. Upon heating, C and H present are oxidised to CO2 and water vapours while N2 is set free. Let, the molecular formula of the organic compound (1 mol) be CxHyNz. In the Dumas method,
{tex}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_x}{{\text{H}}_y}{{\text{N}}_z}}\limits_{1mol} + \left( {2x + \frac{y}{2}} \right){\text{CuO}}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow {/tex}  {tex}x \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\frac{y}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\frac{z}{2} \mathrm{N}_{2}+\left(2 x+\frac{y}{2}\right) \mathrm{Cu}{/tex}
Now, x = 6, {tex}\frac{y}{2}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 8 and {tex}\frac{z}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The molecular formula of the compound is C6H8N2.

questions.explanation: Dichloromethane, DCM (CH2Cl2) is heavier (density = 1.3266 g cm-3) than water (density = 1 g cm-3). So, DCM and H2O will stay as the lower and upper layer respectively in the separating funnel (SF).

questions.question: Which amongst the following is the strongest acid?
questions.question_raw: Which amongst the following is the strongest acid?
questions.explanation:
We know, a stronger acid produces its stable or weaker conjugate base. Here, CH(CN)3 produces the most stable conjugate base (NC)3C-. Stronger -R and -I effects of the CN- group, make the carbanion (conjugate base) very stable. The resonance hybrid structure of [(NC)3C]- is as follows:


However, halogen (X = Cl, Br, I) show - I effect but + R effect of halogens, destabilises the carbanion, X3C- (conjugate base of the haloform, HCA3).

Thus, CH(CN)3 is the strongest acid among the given options.

questions.explanation:
Aromatic compounds are stable due to resonance while non-aromatics are not. According to Huckel’s rule (or 4n + 2 rule), “For a planar, cyclic compound to be aromatic, its {tex}\pi{/tex} cloud must contain (4n + 2){tex}\pi{/tex}electrons, where, n is any whole number.” Thus,

are aromatic and stabilised by resonance. They follow Huckel’s rule.
 is non-aromatic, hence, least stabilised by resonance.

questions.question: In allene (C3H4), the type(s) of hybridization of the carbon atoms, is (are)
questions.question_raw: In allene (C3H4), the type(s) of hybridization of the carbon atoms, is (are)
questions.explanation:
Allene is the name given to propdiene, {tex}H_2C=C=CH_2{/tex}. Allene Hybridisation of an atom is determined by determining the number of hybrid orbitals at that atom which is equal to the number of sigma ({tex}\sigma{/tex}) bonds plus a number of lone pairs at the concerned atom. Pi({tex}\pi{/tex}) bonds are not formed by hybrid orbitals, therefore, not counted for hybridisation.

Here, the terminal carbons have only three sigma bonds associated with them, therefore, hybridisation of terminal carbons is sp3. The central carbon has only two sigma bonds associated, hence hybridisation at central carbon is sp.

questions.question:
The correct stability order of the following resonance structure is
 ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ - $
 ${{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ + - {\text{N}} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ - $
 ${{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^{\,\,\, - } - \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + \equiv {\text{N}}$
 ${{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ - - {\text{N}} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + $
questions.question_raw:
The correct stability order of the following resonance structure is

  1. {tex}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ - {/tex}
  2. {tex}{{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ + - {\text{N}} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ - {/tex}
  3. {tex}{{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {{\text{ C}}}\limits^{\,\,\, - } - \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + \equiv {\text{N}}{/tex}
  4. {tex}{{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ - - {\text{N}} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + {/tex}
questions.explanation:
I is most stable because it has more covalent bonds and negative charge on electronegative nitrogen. Ill is more stable than II and IV due to greater number of covalent bonds. Between II and IV, II is more stable since, it has negative charge on electronegative atom and positive charge on electropositive atom. Hence, overall stability order is
(I) > (III) > (II) > (IV)

questions.question: When metal ‘M' is treated with NaOH, a white gelatinous precipitate ‘A’ is obtained, which is soluble in excess of NaOH. Compound ‘X’ when heated strongly gives an oxide that is used in chromatography as an adsorbent. The metal ‘M' is
questions.question_raw: When metal ‘M' is treated with NaOH, a white gelatinous precipitate ‘A’ is obtained, which is soluble in excess of NaOH. Compound ‘X’ when heated strongly gives an oxide that is used in chromatography as an adsorbent. The metal ‘M' is
questions.explanation:
Among the given metals Al forms white gelatinous ppt. with NaOH.
Hence, the probable metal can be Al. This ppt. is dissolved in excess of NaOH due to the formation of sodium metal Aluminate. Both the reactions are shown below.

{tex}{\text{A}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}}\mathop {{\text{Al(OH}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{White}}\,\,{\text{gelatinous}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{ppt}}{\text{.}}\,{\text{(X)}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{aluminium}}}\limits_{{\text{hydroxide}}} } } \xrightarrow{{{\text{Excess}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{NaOH}}}}\mathop {{\text{NaAl}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{(Sodium}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{metaluminate}}}\limits_{{\text{soluble}}} } }{/tex} 
Aluminium hydroxide on strong heating gives alumina (Al2O3) which is used as an adsorbent in chromatography. This reaction can be seen as :
{tex}2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}
Thus, metal M is Al.
Ca, being below sodium in electrochemical reactivity series, cannot displaces Na from its aqueous solution.
Zn reacts with NaOH to form sodium zincate which is a soluble compound.
Fe reacts with sodium hydroxide to form tetrahydroferrate (II) sodium which is again a soluble complex.

questions.question: In Carius method of estimation of halogens 250 mg of an organic compound gave 141 mg of AgBr. The percentage of bromine in the compound is (atomic mass Ag = 108, Br = 80)
questions.question_raw: In Carius method of estimation of halogens 250 mg of an organic compound gave 141 mg of AgBr. The percentage of bromine in the compound is (atomic mass Ag = 108, Br = 80)
questions.explanation:
Given, Weight of organic compound = 250 mg
Weight of AgBr = 141 mg
{tex}\therefore{/tex} According to the formula of 10% of bromine by Carius method
% of Br = {tex}\frac{Atomic \ weight \ of \ Br}{Molecular \ weight \ of \ AgBr}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{Weight \ of \ AgBr}{Weight \ of \ organic \ bromide} \times 100{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % of Br = {tex}\frac{80}{188} \times \frac{141}{250} \times 100=\frac{1128000}{47000}=24 \%{/tex}

questions.question:
The correct order of basicities of the following compounds is
 CH3CH2NH2
 (CH3)2NH
 CH3CONH2
questions.question_raw:
The correct order of basicities of the following compounds is


  2. CH3CH2NH2
  3. (CH3)2NH
  4. CH3CONH2
questions.explanation:
I is most basic due to formation of resonance stabilised conjugate acid.

IV is amide, least basic.
{tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{||} }\limits^O - \ddot N{H_2} \leftrightarrow {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^| }\limits^{{O^ - }} = \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + {{\text{H}}_2}}\limits_{lone\,pair\,is\,part\,of\,delocalisation} {/tex}
Also, among alkyl amines, 2o is more basic than 1o amine. Hence, overall order of basic strength is
1 > 3 > 2 > 4

questions.question: X and Fare two elements which form X2Y3 and X3Y4. If 0.20 mol of X2Y3 weighs 32.0 g and 0.4 mol of X3Y4 weighs 92.8 g, the atomic weights of X and Y Fare respectively

Let atomic wt. of X = xgmol-1 Atomic wt. of Y = ygmol-1
{tex}\frac{32}{2 x+3 y}=0.2 ; \quad \frac{92.8}{3 x+4 y}=0.4{/tex}

questions.question: 1.0 g of a monobasic acid when completely acted upon Mg gave 1.301 g of anhydrous Mg salt. Equivalent weight of acid is
questions.question_raw: 1.0 g of a monobasic acid when completely acted upon Mg gave 1.301 g of anhydrous Mg salt. Equivalent weight of acid is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{E w \text { of acid }}{\text { Ewof salt of } M g}=\frac{\text { weight of acid }}{\text { Weight of slt }}{/tex} Mg + 2HCl (monobasic acid) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} MgCl2 + H2
Let Ew of acid = Ew of H + Ew of acid radical
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ew of salt of Mg = Ew of Mg + Ew of acid radical
{tex}\therefore \frac{E w \text { of acid }}{E w o f M g \text { salt }}=\frac{\text { weight of acid }}{\text {Weight of Mgsalt}} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{E w o f H+E w \text { of acid radical }(E)}{\text { Ew of } M g+\text { Ewof acid radical }(E)}=\frac{1.0}{1.301} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1+E}{12+E}=\frac{1.0}{1.301}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E = 35 , 54
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EW of acid = Ew of H + Ew of acid radical =1+35.54=36.54

questions.question: If 0.5 mole of BaCl2 is mixed with 0.20 mole of Na3PO4, the maximum number of moles of Ba3(PO4) that can be formed is
questions.question_raw: If 0.5 mole of BaCl2 is mixed with 0.20 mole of Na3PO4, the maximum number of moles of Ba3(PO4) that can be formed is
questions.explanation:
BaCl2 + 2Na3PO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6NaCl + Ba3(PO4)2 [3 mol 2 mol 6 mol 1 mol]
Given {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.5 mol of BaCl2 and 0.2 mol of Na3PO4
To find the limiting reagent: 2 mol of Na3PO4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 mol of BaCl2 0.2 mol of Na3PO4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.3 mol of BaCl2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Na3PO4 is the limiting reagent
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 mol of Na3PO4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 mol of Ba3(PO4)2
0.2 mol of Na3PO4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 mol of Ba3(PO4)2

questions.question: The simplest formula of a compound containing 50% of an element X (atomic weight 10) and 50% of element Y (atomic weight 20) is:
questions.question_raw: The simplest formula of a compound containing 50% of an element X (atomic weight 10) and 50% of element Y (atomic weight 20) is:
questions.explanation:
In this, we have to find the empirical formula
So  for X =50/10=5
And for Y= 50/20=5/2
Dividing these value with 5/2
We get ratio

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs represents stereoisomerism?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following pairs represents stereoisomerism?
questions.explanation:
Stereoisomers are isomeric molecules that have the same molecular formula and sequence of bonded atoms but differ in the three-dimensional orientations of their atoms in space.
Pair of optical isomerism and geometrical isomerism are able to exhibit the phenomenon of stereoisomerism because both types of isomers differ only in their orientation in space.

Percentage of chlorine in AgCl

questions.question:
Which of the following is not correct?
 A sigma bond is weaker than $\pi$ bond
 A sigma bond is stronger than $\pi$ bond
 A double bond is stronger than a single bond
 A double bond is shorter than a single bond
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following is not correct?

  1. A sigma bond is weaker than {tex}\pi{/tex} bond
  2. A sigma bond is stronger than {tex}\pi{/tex} bond
  3. A double bond is stronger than a single bond
  4. A double bond is shorter than a single bond
questions.explanation:
A sigma bond is formed by end-to-end overlap of the bonding orbitals. Whereas, a {tex}\pi{/tex}-bond is formed by side-to-side overlap of (usually) two p-orbitals. This places the electron density above and below the plane of the nuclei with a node in the middle. The nodal region reduces the degree to which the orbitals overlap, and therefore, a pi bond is weaker than a sigma bond between the same two elements.
Hydrogen bonds are a strong type of dipole-dipole interaction. As a Rule of Thumb, they are weaker than covalent and ionic ("intramolecular") bonds", but stronger than most dipole-dipole interactions.

  4. CH2=CH-CH=CH2

questions.explanation: The decrease in C-C {tex}{\sigma}{/tex} bond length as C hybridization changes from sp3 to sp2 to sp reflects the decrease in the bond length. The shortening of all these bonds is a consequence of the adjacent double and triple bonds. The carbon-carbon single bond of the alkyne is shorter than alkene and those of alkane. The order of bond length is iii > iv > i > ii.

questions.question:
Arrange the following compounds in order of increasing dipole moment:
Toluene (I)
m-dichlorobenzene (II)
o-dichlorobenzene (III)
p-dichlorobenzene (IV)
questions.question_raw:
Arrange the following compounds in order of increasing dipole moment:
Toluene (I)
m-dichlorobenzene (II)
o-dichlorobenzene (III)
p-dichlorobenzene (IV)
questions.explanation:
The increasing order of dipole moment is IV < I < II < III
o−, m−, p− derivates has {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 60o, 120o and 180o and thus, resultant vector has zero dipole moment in p-derivative because of symmetrical structure. Also dipole moment of m-dichlorobenzene is more than toluene. o− and m−dichlorobenzene have higher dipole moments than toluene due to high electronegativity of chlorine than CH3​ group. Further, to the o-dichlorobenzene has higher dipole moment due to lower bond angle than the m-isomer.

questions.question: A flask contains a mixture of isohexane and 3-methylpentane. One of the liquids boils at 63oC while the other boils at 60oC. What is the best way to separate the two liquids and which one will be distilled out first?
questions.question_raw: A flask contains a mixture of isohexane and 3-methylpentane. One of the liquids boils at 63oC while the other boils at 60oC. What is the best way to separate the two liquids and which one will be distilled out first?
questions.explanation: A liquid having a lower boiling point comes out first in fractional distillation. Simple distillation can’t be used as the boiling point difference is very small. 3-Methylpantane will show a greater boiling point (63oC) comparative to isohexane due to symmetrical structure. Therefore isohexane distilled out first.

II moves faster and has higher Rf value than I
I moves faster and has higher Rf value than II

questions.explanation: Since, adsorption of I > II, I is firmly attached to the column (stationary phase). Hence, it moves slowly and will cover little distance, while II is loosely attached to the column (stationary phase). Hence, it moves faster and will cover a large distance.

O - H bond undergoes cleavage most readily because O and H differ markedly in their electronegativity and further oxygen being highly electronegative can accommodate the negative charge more effectively developed after the cleavage.

questions.question:
In which of the following pairs A is more stable than B?
 A
 B
 (i) 
 (ii) 
 (iii) 
 (iv) $\mathrm{Ph}_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\bullet}$
 $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\bullet}$
questions.question_raw:
In which of the following pairs A is more stable than B?

A B
(i) 
(ii) 
(iii) 
(iv) {tex}\mathrm{Ph}_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\bullet}{/tex} {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}^{\bullet}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
A: Cyclohex-1-enyl radical vs. B: Cyclohex-2-enyl radical

  • The radical in A is at the allylic position (delocalized over the double bond), making it more stable due to resonance.
  • In B, the radical is at a vinylic position, which lacks resonance stabilization.
  • A is more stable than B.

A: Triphenylmethyl radical (Ph₃C•) vs. B: Tert-butyl radical ((CH₃)₃C•)

  • The Ph₃C• radical is highly stabilized due to extensive resonance with the three benzene rings.
  • The tert-butyl radical is only stabilized by hyperconjugation.
  • A is much more stable than B

questions.question: Complete combustion of 2.24 litre (at NTP) of a hydrocarbon produces 4.4 g carbon dioxide and 3.6 g H2O. Find the molecular formula of the compound.
questions.question_raw: Complete combustion of 2.24 litre (at NTP) of a hydrocarbon produces 4.4 g carbon dioxide and 3.6 g H2O. Find the molecular formula of the compound.
questions.explanation:
2.24 litre of hydrocarbon = {tex}\frac{2.24}{22.4}{/tex} = 0.1mol 
Moles of CO2 formed = {tex}\frac{4.4 \text{g}}{44 \text{g} \text{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.1 mol
Moles of H2O formed = {tex}\frac{3.6 \text{g}}{18 \text{g} \text{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.2 mol
If the molecular formula of the hydrocarbon is CaHb, the combustion reaction can be written as:
CaHb + {tex}\left(\text{a}+\frac{\text{b}}{4}\right) \text{O}_{2} \longrightarrow{/tex} a CO2 + {tex}\frac{\text{b}}{2}{/tex} H2O
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mol of CaHb will gives = a mol CO2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1 mol of CaHb will give = (0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} a) mol of CO2
0.1 = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mol of CaHb gives {tex}\frac{\text{b}}{2}{/tex} mol H2O
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1 mol of CaHb will give = {tex}\left(0.1 \times \frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}\right){/tex} mol of H2O
0.1 {tex}\times \frac{\text{b}}{2}{/tex} = 0.2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} b = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Hydrocarbon is CH4

questions.question:
Which of the following order shows the correct decreasing order of heat of hydrogenation?
 CH2 = CH2
 CH3 - CH = CH2
 CH3 - CH = CH - CH3
 $CH_3 - \overset {\overset {CH_3}|}C = CH - CH_3$
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following order shows the correct decreasing order of heat of hydrogenation?

  1. CH2 = CH2
  2. CH3 - CH = CH2
  3. CH3 - CH = CH - CH3
  4. {tex}CH_3 - \overset {\overset {CH_3}|}C = CH - CH_3{/tex}
questions.explanation:
The greater the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogens, the greater would be the stability of alkenes. Thus, a greater extent of hyperconjugation results in a lower value of heat of hydrogenation. Hence, correct decreasing order is,
CH2 = CH2 > CH3 - CH = CH2 > CH3 - CH = CH - CH3 > CH3 - {tex}\overset {\overset {CH_3}|}C{/tex} = CH - CH3

questions.question: The ratio of mass percent of C and H of an organic compound (CxHyOz) is 6 : 1. If one molecule of the above compound (CxHyOz) contains half as much oxygen as required to burn one molecule of compound CxHy completely to CO2 and H2O. The empirical formula of compound CxHyOz is ________.
questions.question_raw: The ratio of mass percent of C and H of an organic compound (CxHyOz) is 6 : 1. If one molecule of the above compound (CxHyOz) contains half as much oxygen as required to burn one molecule of compound CxHy completely to CO2 and H2O. The empirical formula of compound CxHyOz is ________.
questions.explanation:
The ratio of mass percent of C and H in CxHyOz = 6 : 1
This means, {tex}\frac{12 X}{Y}=\frac{6}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2X = Y for CXHYOZ
Now,
CXHY + {tex}\left(\mathrm{X}+\frac{\mathrm{Y}}{4}\right){/tex}O2 {tex}\to{/tex} XCO2 + {tex}\left(\frac{Y}{2}\right){/tex}H2O
In CXHYOZ, number of O - atoms is Z.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of oxygen atoms required for CXHY combustion is,
{tex}\left(\mathrm{X}+\frac{\mathrm{Y}}{4}\right) \times 2=\left(2 \mathrm{X}+\frac{\mathrm{Y}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Z = {tex}\frac{3 \mathrm{X}}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of X : Y : Z = X : 2X : {tex}\frac{3 \mathrm{X}}{2}{/tex} = 2 : 4 : 3
Hence, the empirical formula of the compound CXHYOZ is C2H4O3.

questions.question: The most stable carbocation, among the following is ________.
questions.question_raw: The most stable carbocation, among the following is ________.
questions.explanation:
The stability of carbocations depends upon the extent of the hyperconjugation effect which in turn depends on the number of hyperconjugation structures possible.
A number of hyperconjugation structures = No. of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-H atoms adjacent to sp2 hybridized C-atom.
(Greater the number of hyperconjugation structures, greater will be the stability.),
{tex}\overset {\alpha -H}{CH_3} -\overset + CH  - \overset {\alpha -H}{CH_2} - CH_2 - CH_3{/tex}
has a maximum (five) {tex}\alpha{/tex}-H and thus has five hyperconjugation structures. Hence, it is the most stable carbocation among the given.

questions.explanation: Steam distillation is essentially co-distillation with water and is carried out when a substance is insoluble in water and is volatile with steam but impurities are non-volatile. In steam distillation, the liquid mixture boils when the sum of vapour pressures due to the organic liquid (p1) and that due to water (p2) becomes equal to the atmospheric pressure (p), i.e., p = p1 + p2. Since p1 is lower than p, the organic liquid vaporizes at lower temperature than its boiling point.

questions.explanation:

V0 = volume of N2 at STP in mL.
Approximate % of nitrogen = {tex}\frac{\text{V}_0}{\text{w}} \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{45}{0.3} \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex} = 18.75 % of N

questions.question: NH3 evolved from 0.62 g of organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method was absorbed in 82 mL of $\frac 14$ M H2SO4. The excess of acid neutralized by the addition of 140 mL of $\frac 14$ M NaOH. What is the percentage of nitrogen in the substance?
questions.question_raw: NH3 evolved from 0.62 g of organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method was absorbed in 82 mL of {tex}\frac 14{/tex} M H2SO4. The excess of acid neutralized by the addition of 140 mL of {tex}\frac 14{/tex} M NaOH. What is the percentage of nitrogen in the substance?
questions.explanation:
Volume of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} H2SO4 taken = 82 mL
Let us first calculate the volume of excess {tex}\frac M4{/tex} H2SO4 which was neutralized by 140 mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} NaOH
140 mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} NaOH = {tex}\frac{140}{2}{/tex}mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} H2SO4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} H2SO4 left unused = 70 mL
Volume of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} H2SO4 used for neutralisation of NH3 = 82 - 70 = 12 mL
Now, 12 mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} H2SO4
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} NH3 = 24 mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} NH3
1000 mL of 1 M NH3 contain 14 g nitrogen.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 24 mL of {tex}\frac M4{/tex} NH3 contain
= {tex}\frac{14}{1000} \times \frac{24 \times 1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage of nitrogen = {tex}\frac{14 \times 24 \times 100}{1000 \times 4 \times 0.62}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{33600}{2480}{/tex} = 13.54 %

questions.question: An organic compound having C, H and O has 13.13% H, 52.14% C. Its molar mass is 46.068 g mol-1. What are its empirical and molecular formulae respectively?
questions.question_raw: An organic compound having C, H and O has 13.13% H, 52.14% C. Its molar mass is 46.068 g mol-1. What are its empirical and molecular formulae respectively?
questions.explanation:
% of carbon = 52.14
% of hydrogen = 13.13
% of oxygen = 100 - 52.14 - 13.13
= 34.73

Element Atomic Ratio Simplest Ratio
C = 52.14% {tex}\frac {52.14}{12}{/tex} = 4.345 {tex}\frac {4.315}{2.171}{/tex} = 2
H = 13.13% {tex}\frac {13.13}{1}{/tex} = 13.13 {tex}\frac {13.13}{2.171}{/tex} = 6
O = 34.73% {tex}\frac {34.73}{16}{/tex} = 2.171 {tex}\frac {2.171}{2.171}{/tex} = 1

Thus, the empirical formula is C2H6O.
Empirical formula mass = 46
Molecular formula = n {tex}\times{/tex} empirical formula
n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{46.068}{46}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molecular formula = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} (C2H6O) = C2H6O

questions.question: State the correct increasing order of stability of free radicals.
questions.question_raw: State the correct increasing order of stability of free radicals.
questions.explanation:
On the basis of the hyper conjugative effect of the alkyl groups, the stability of free radicals follows the order, 3° > 2° > 1°. Benzyl free radicals are stabilized by resonance and hence are more stable than alkyl free radicals. In addition, the more the number of phenyl groups attached to the carbon atom (with the odd electron), the more is the stability of the corresponding free radical.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CH3 {tex}\overset {\bullet }CH_2{/tex} < (CH3)2 {tex}\overset {\bullet }CH{/tex} < C6H5{tex}\overset {\bullet }CH_2{/tex} < (CH3)3 {tex}\overset {\bullet }C{/tex} < (C6H5)2 {tex}\overset {\bullet }CH{/tex}

questions.question: How many metamers of hexan-3-one are possible?
questions.question_raw: How many metamers of hexan-3-one are possible?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Hexan-3-one}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-CH_2-CH_3}{/tex}
{tex}\underset{Hexan-2-one}{CH_3-\underset O{\underset{\vert\vert}C}-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}{/tex} ... (i)
{tex}\underset{4-Methylpen\tan-2-one}{CH_3-\underset O{\underset{\vert\vert}C}-CH_2-\overset{CH_3}{\overset\vert{CH}}-CH_3}{/tex} ... (ii)
{tex}\underset{3-Methylpen\tan-2-one}{CH_3-\underset O{\underset{\vert\vert}C}-\overset{CH_3}{\overset\vert{CH}}-CH_2-CH_3}{/tex} ... (iii)
{tex}\underset{2-Methylpen\tan-3-one}{CH_3-\overset{CH_3}{\overset\vert{CH}}-\underset O{\underset{\vert\vert}C}-CH_2-CH_3}{/tex} ... (iv)
{tex}\underset{3,3-Dimethylbu\tan-2-one}{CH_3-\underset O{\underset{\vert\vert}C}-\underset{CH_3}{\underset\vert{\overset{CH_3}{\overset\vert C}}}-CH_3}{/tex} ... (v)
Hence, there are 5 metamers with varied number of alkyl groups on either sides of the functional group.

questions.question: A hydrocarbon has 75% C. How much CO2 in g will be obtained on complete combustion of 0.8 g of hydrocarbon?
questions.question_raw: A hydrocarbon has 75% C. How much CO2 in g will be obtained on complete combustion of 0.8 g of hydrocarbon?
questions.explanation:
% of carbon = {tex}\frac{m}{w}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 27
75 = {tex}\frac{m}{0.8}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 27
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of CO2 produced = 2.2 g

questions.question: In Dumas method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.25 g of an organic compound gave 40 mL of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 725 mm pressure. If the aqueous tension at 300 K is 25 mm, the percentage of nitrogen in the compound is ________.
questions.question_raw: In Dumas method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.25 g of an organic compound gave 40 mL of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 725 mm pressure. If the aqueous tension at 300 K is 25 mm, the percentage of nitrogen in the compound is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, m = 0.25 g, P = 725 mm, f = 25 mm, V1 = 40 mL, T1 = 300 K, P0 = 760 mm, T0 = 273 K
Here P0 and T0 are the pressure and temperature of dry nitrogen at STP respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P1 = P - f = 725 - 25 = 700 mm
{tex}\frac{P_{0} V_{0}}{T_{0}}=\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V0 = {tex}\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}} \times \frac{T_{0}}{P_{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{700 \times 40}{300} \times \frac{273}{760}{/tex}
= 33.53 mL
% of nitrogen = {tex}\frac{{V}_{0}}{{w}} \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{33.53}{0.25} \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex}
= 16.76% of N

questions.question: In Kjeldahl’s method for estimation of nitrogen present in a soil sample, ammonia evolved from 0.75 g of sample neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. The percentage of nitrogen in the soil is ________.
questions.question_raw: In Kjeldahl’s method for estimation of nitrogen present in a soil sample, ammonia evolved from 0.75 g of sample neutralized 10 mL of 1 M H2SO4. The percentage of nitrogen in the soil is ________.
questions.explanation:
10 mL of 1 M H2SO4 = 20 mL of 1 M NH3
1000 mL of 1 M NH3 contains 14g of N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 20 mL of 1 M NH3 contains {tex}\frac{14 \times 20}{1000}{/tex}g of N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % of nitrogen = {tex}\frac{14 \times 20}{1000 \times 0.75} \times 100{/tex} = 37.33%

questions.question: An organic compound contains C = 72%, H = 12% and the rest is oxygen. Empirical formula of the compound is ________.
questions.question_raw: An organic compound contains C = 72%, H = 12% and the rest is oxygen. Empirical formula of the compound is ________.
questions.explanation:
% of carbon = 72%
% of hydrogen = 12%
% of oxygen = 100 - (72 + 12) = 16

Element Atomic ratio Simplest ratio
C = 72% {tex}\frac{72}{12}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\frac{6}{1}{/tex} = 6
H = 12% {tex}\frac{12}{1}{/tex} = 12 {tex}\frac{12}{1}{/tex} = 6
O = 16% {tex}\frac{16}{16}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = 1

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Empirical formula of the compound is C6H12O.

questions.question: Empirical formula of a compound is C2H6O2 and its molecular mass is 124. Molecular formula of the compound is ________.
questions.question_raw: Empirical formula of a compound is C2H6O2 and its molecular mass is 124. Molecular formula of the compound is ________.
questions.explanation:
n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}=\frac{124}{62}{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molecular formula = n {tex}\times{/tex} empirical formula
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} C2H6O2
= C4H12O4

questions.question: An organic compound contains C, H and O in the proportion of 12 : 2 : 16 by weight respectively. Its vapour density is 30. Molecular formula of the compound is ________.
questions.question_raw: An organic compound contains C, H and O in the proportion of 12 : 2 : 16 by weight respectively. Its vapour density is 30. Molecular formula of the compound is ________.
questions.explanation:
Elements No. of moles Simplest ratio
C {tex}\frac{12}{12}{/tex} = 1 1
H {tex}\frac{2}{1}{/tex} = 1 2
O {tex}\frac{16}{16}{/tex} = 1 1

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Empirical formula = CH2O
Empirical formula mass = 12 + 2 + 16 = 30
Molecular mass = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} vapour density
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 30
= 60
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{60}{30}{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molecular formula = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} CH2O = C2H4O2

questions.question: State the correct order of stability for the following carbocations:
questions.question_raw: State the correct order of stability for the following carbocations:
questions.explanation: Stability of carbocation increases with the introduction of electron donating groups (+1 effect), i.e., here -CH3 group is the electron donor. This -CH3 group along with the resonance of the benzyl group contributes to the stability of carbocations. Ortho and para methyl substituents are electron donating and hence they are the most stable followed by meta and the one without any substituent would be least stable of all. In between ortho and para substituents, it is the para-substituted carbocation that is more stable as the orthosubstituted carbocation is stearically hindered.

questions.question: Predict the most stable carbocation amongst the following.
questions.question_raw: Predict the most stable carbocation amongst the following.
questions.explanation:
F shows -I effect
NH2 shows +M effect (more effective than +1)
CN shows -M effect and -I effect
NO2 shows -M effect and -I effect
The presence of electron-donating groups such as -OH, -NH2, etc. in benzene increases the stability of carbocation whereas the presence of electron-withdrawing groups such as -NO2, -CN, -Cl, etc. decreases the stability of carbocation.

questions.question: The decreasing order of strength of the following bases is ________.
questions.question_raw: The decreasing order of strength of the following bases is ________.
questions.explanation:
Their conjugate acids are CH2 = CH - CH2 - H, CH3 - CH3, H - OH. Their acidic character is, H - OH > CH2 = CH - CH3 > CH3 - CH3.
A strong acid has a weak conjugate base.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The decreasing order of base strength is, CH3 - {tex}\overset -CH_2{/tex} > CH2 = CH - {tex}\overset -CH_2 > \overset -OH{/tex}

questions.explanation:
Rf value for carotene = {tex}\frac{98}{105}{/tex} = 0.93
Rf value for xanthophyll = {tex}\frac{30}{105}{/tex} = 0.286
Rf value for chlorophyll-a = {tex}\frac{25}{105}{/tex} = 0.238
Rf value for chlorophyll-b = {tex}\frac{13}{105}{/tex} = 0.124
Since, Rf value for carotene is the highest, therefore it is weakly adsorbed as compared to other pigments. Hence, carotene elutes first from the mixture of pigments on its isolation using chromatography.

questions.question:
State the correct order of stability for the following carbocations,
 CH3 - O - $\overset + {\text C \text H}_2$
 CH3 - NH - $\overset + {\text C \text H}_2$
 CH3 - S - $\overset + {\text C \text H}_2$​​​​​
questions.question_raw:
State the correct order of stability for the following carbocations,

  1. CH3 - O - {tex}\overset + {\text C \text H}_2{/tex}
  2. CH3 - NH - {tex}\overset + {\text C \text H}_2{/tex}
  3. CH3 - S - {tex}\overset + {\text C \text H}_2{/tex}​​​​​
questions.explanation:
A carbocation is electron-deficient and to form a stable carbocation, there should be an electropositive element adjacent to the carbon so that it pushes electrons towards carbon. In the case of O, N and S, oxygen is highly electronegative followed by nitrogen and sulphur respectively.
By this reasoning, sulphur is the least electronegative element that should best stabilize the carbocation. But it is found that nitrogen stabilizes the carbocation and this is due to the mesomeric effect (N can easily donate its electrons compared to O).
 {tex}\longleftrightarrow \text{CH}_{3}-\overset + {\text{NH}}=\text{CH}_{2}{/tex}
This resonance stabilization is less prevalent with sulphur as its p-orbitals being large in size cannot overlap effectively with that of carbon.

questions.question:
Which of the following orders is CORRECT for the nucleophilicity of these anions?
$CH_3-\overset {\overset {O}{||}}C-O^-$
OH-
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following orders is CORRECT for the nucleophilicity of these anions?

  2. {tex}CH_3-\overset {\overset {O}{||}}C-O^-{/tex}
  3. OH-
questions.explanation:
Nucleophilicity {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { delocalisation of lone pair }}{/tex}
{tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { stability of anions }}{/tex}
In structure (i), the negative charge is delocalized over the ring due to resonance. However, resonating structures are nonequivalent (with a negative charge on carbon atoms of the ring). In structure (ii), the negative charge is delocalized due to resonance. However, resonating structures are equivalent to a negative charge on O-atoms. In structure (iii), there is no delocalization. Hence, the stability of anions increases in the order: iii > i > ii
Thus, the decreasing order of nucleophilicity is iii > i > ii

questions.question: Two students X and Y were asked to determine the elements present in an organic compound (in addition to C and H) given to each one of them by Lassaigne’s test. Both of them first prepared the Lassaigne’s extract by fusing the given compound with sodium metal in a fusion tube and then added silver nitrate to their respective extracts after acidifying it with nitric acid. X observed a dark yellow precipitate in his test tube whereas Y observed a white precipitate. Both were surprised to see two different colours and reported it to their guide. They were then asked to add ammonium hydroxide solution wherein X found his prepared precipitate to be insoluble and Y found his prepared precipitate to be soluble. Identify the elements present in their respective compounds?
questions.question_raw: Two students X and Y were asked to determine the elements present in an organic compound (in addition to C and H) given to each one of them by Lassaigne’s test. Both of them first prepared the Lassaigne’s extract by fusing the given compound with sodium metal in a fusion tube and then added silver nitrate to their respective extracts after acidifying it with nitric acid. X observed a dark yellow precipitate in his test tube whereas Y observed a white precipitate. Both were surprised to see two different colours and reported it to their guide. They were then asked to add ammonium hydroxide solution wherein X found his prepared precipitate to be insoluble and Y found his prepared precipitate to be soluble. Identify the elements present in their respective compounds?
questions.explanation:
X observed a dark yellow precipitate in his test tube and this is due to presence of iodine in his compound.

The presence of iodine is confirmed by the insolubility of yellow precipitate in ammonium hydroxide solution. If it was bromine, then the yellow precipitate will be sparingly soluble in ammonium hydroxide. Y observed a white precipitate in his test tube and this is due to the presence of chlorine in his compound.

questions.question_raw:
The total number of compounds having a functional group with at least two hetero atoms is ________.

  1. Acetaldehyde
  2. Formic acid
  3. Diethyl ether
  4. Methylamine
  5. Nitromethane
  6. Acetonitrile
  7. Acetamide
  8. Acetic anhydride
questions.explanation:
To determine the total number of compounds with a functional group containing at least two hetero atoms, we need to identify the compounds with functional groups meeting this criterion. Hetero atoms are atoms other than carbon and hydrogen, such as oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and others.

Let's examine each compound:

  1. Acetaldehyde (CH3CHO): Contains one hetero atom (oxygen) in the aldehyde functional group.

  2. Formic acid (HCOOH): Contains two hetero atoms (two oxygens) in the carboxylic acid functional group.

  3. Diethyl ether (C2H5OC2H5): Contains one hetero atom (oxygen) in the ether functional group.

  4. Methylamine (CH3NH2): Contains one hetero atom (nitrogen) in the amine functional group.

  5. Nitromethane (CH3NO2): Contains two hetero atoms (one oxygen and one nitrogen) in the nitro functional group.

  6. Acetonitrile (CH3CN): Contains one hetero atom (nitrogen) in the nitrile functional group.

  7. Acetamide (CH3CONH2): Contains two hetero atoms (one oxygen and one nitrogen) in the amide functional group.

  8. Acetic anhydride (CH3COOCOCH3): Contains two hetero atoms (two oxygens) in the anhydride functional group.

Now, let's count the compounds with functional groups containing at least two hetero atoms:

  • Formic acid
  • Nitromethane
  • Acetamide
  • Acetic anhydride

There are 4 compounds that meet the criterion of having a functional group with at least two hetero atoms.

Given
In the above compounds, correct order of reactivity in electrophilic substitution reactions will be:
questions.question_raw:
Given

In the above compounds, correct order of reactivity in electrophilic substitution reactions will be:
questions.explanation:
-Cl and -CH3 groups are o and p directing. They are electron releasing due to +M and hyperconjugation effects. Further, since such groups increase electron density in the nucleus, they facilitate further electrophilic substitution and hence, known as activating group. The activating effect of these groups is in order of -CH3 > -X but chlorine exceptionally deactive the ring due to strong -1 effect. Hence, it is difficult to carry out substitution in chlorobenzene than in benzene.
Further, -NO2 is a deactivating group, hence deactivates the benzene nucleus, i.e., hinder the further substitution. Thus, nitrobenzene undergo electrophilic substitution with a great difficulty.
Hence, the correct order will be:

The order of halogens as departing nucleophile should be:
questions.question_raw:
In nucleophilic substitution reaction, order of halogens as incoming (attacking) nucleophile is: I- > Br- > Cl- 
The order of halogens as departing nucleophile should be:
questions.explanation:
Since the leaving group breaks away as a base, it is easier to displace weaker bases as compared to stronger bases. Thus less basic the substituent, the more easily it is displaced.
The basic strength of the given groups is in order:
I- < Br- < Cl-
Thus, the order of halogen leaving groups is:
I- > Br- > Cl-

The most stable carbocation is t-alkyl carbocation because the order of stability of alkyl carbocation is 
t-alkyl > s-alkyl > p-alkly > CH3 carbocation
The stability order is described with the help of hyperconjugation and inductive effect. On the basis of hyper-conjugation
{tex}{({\mathrm{CH}}_3)}_2{\overset+{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}\overset1{\mathrm C}{\mathrm H}_2\;{/tex} {tex}=\overset2{\mathrm C}\mathrm H-{\overset3{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}_2-{\overset4{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}_2-\overset5{\mathrm C}\equiv\overset6{\mathrm C}\mathrm H{/tex}
shows six resonating structures due to the presence of six a-C-H bonds.
{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\mathrm H}{\underset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}}-\overset+{\underset{\mathrm H}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}-\mathrm H{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow\mathrm H-\overset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset{\mathrm H^+}{\mathrm C}}}=\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm H{/tex} {tex}\longleftrightarrow\mathrm H^+-\overset{\mathrm H}{\underset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}}=\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm H{/tex} {tex}\longleftrightarrow\mathrm H-\overset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset{\mathrm H^+}{\mathrm C}}}=\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset\vert{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm H{/tex} etc.
{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset{\vert+}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}}{/tex} shows nine resonating structures due to the presence of nine {tex}\alpha{/tex}-C-H bonds. Greater the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen atom greater will be the hyperconjugation and thus greater will be the stability.
{tex}{\overset+{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}_3{/tex} does not show any hyperconjugation while {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\overset+{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}_2{/tex} shows three hyperconjugating structures. The larger number of hyperconjugation is possible in option ({tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset{\vert+}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}}{/tex}) only.
Moreover the +I effect of -CH3 group increase the stability of carbocation by decreasing the magnitude of positive charge.

questions.question_raw: Which of the following is least reactive in a nucleophilic substitution reaction?
questions.explanation:
Chlorine of vinyl chloride (CH2=CHCI) is non-reactive substitution reaction because it shows the following resonating structure due to +M effect of -CI atom.
{tex}\longleftrightarrow{/tex} {tex} \stackrel{-\boldsymbol{\delta}}{\mathrm{CH}_2}-\underset{\text { (II) }}{\mathrm{CH}}=\stackrel{-\delta}{\mathrm{Cl}}{/tex}
In (II) Cl-atom has positive charge and partial double bond character with C of vinyl group, so it is more tightly attracted towards the nucleus and it does not get replaced by nucleophile in nucleophilic substitution reaction.

questions.question:

$\underset7{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset\vert{\underset{6\vert\;}{\mathrm C}}}}-\underset5{\mathrm C}\mathrm H=\underset4{\mathrm C}\mathrm H-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset3{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}\mathrm H}-\underset2{\mathrm C}\equiv\underset1{\mathrm{CH}}$
is in the following sequence:
questions.question_raw:
The state of hybridisation of C2, C3, C5 and C6 of the hydrocarbon,
{tex}\underset7{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset\vert{\underset{6\vert\;}{\mathrm C}}}}-\underset5{\mathrm C}\mathrm H=\underset4{\mathrm C}\mathrm H-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset3{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}\mathrm H}-\underset2{\mathrm C}\equiv\underset1{\mathrm{CH}}{/tex}
is in the following sequence:
questions.explanation:
Count a number of sigma bond and then find hybridization as follows.
In case of carbon atom if:

  • It attaches to {tex}2\sigma{/tex} - bonds then hybridization is sp
  • It attaches to {tex}3\sigma{/tex} - bonds then hybridization is sp2
  • It attaches to {tex}4\sigma{/tex} - bonds then hybridization is sp3
    C2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sp, C3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sp3, C5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sp2, and C6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sp3

    Double and triple bond is not considered while finding hybridization.

questions.question: Which of the following halides will be most reactive in $S_{N^{2}}$ reaction and $S_{N^{1}}$ reaction, respectively?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following halides will be most reactive in {tex}S_{N^{2}}{/tex} reaction and {tex}S_{N^{1}}{/tex} reaction, respectively?
questions.explanation: The solution identifies the most reactive halides for both S{tex}_N{/tex}2 and S{tex}_N{/tex}1 reactions based on the structure of the halides provided. For S{tex}_N{/tex}2 reactions, the reactivity is influenced by steric hindrance. Primary halides are more reactive than secondary or tertiary halides because they have less steric hindrance. In this case, halide I is likely a primary halide, making it the most reactive in an S{tex}_N{/tex}2 reaction. For S{tex}_N{/tex}1 reactions, the reactivity is determined by the stability of the carbocation formed during the reaction. Tertiary halides are more reactive than secondary or primary halides due to the greater stability of tertiary carbocations. Halide III is likely a tertiary halide, making it the most reactive in an S{tex}_N{/tex}1 reaction. Thus, the solution correctly identifies halide I as the most reactive for S{tex}_N{/tex}2 and halide III for S{tex}_N{/tex}1.

questions.question: Mechanism involved and product obtained when 3-iodo-3-ethylpentane is treated with sodium methoxide in methanol is :
questions.question_raw: Mechanism involved and product obtained when 3-iodo-3-ethylpentane is treated with sodium methoxide in methanol is :
questions.explanation: The reaction of 3-iodo-3-ethylpentane with sodium methoxide in methanol primarily leads to an elimination reaction rather than a substitution. Sodium methoxide is a strong base, which promotes the elimination of the iodide ion and the formation of a double bond. In this case, the structure of 3-iodo-3-ethylpentane allows for the formation of a stable alkene through the elimination of the leaving group (iodide) and a hydrogen atom from an adjacent carbon. The mechanism is E2 because it involves a concerted process where the base abstracts a proton while the leaving group departs, leading to the formation of the alkene in a single step. Thus, the product obtained is an alkene, and the mechanism involved is E2.

This stabilization occurs due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects, which can actually increase the rate of the SN1 reaction rather than decrease it. Therefore, the assertion that a CH3 substitution at the {tex}\alpha{/tex} position decreases the rate of SN1 is not accurate.

questions.question: Driving force for Finkelstein reaction is :
questions.question_raw: Driving force for Finkelstein reaction is :

options.text: NaI is a very good nucleophile
NaI is a very good leaving group
Formation of ppt. faciliates forward reaction

questions.explanation: The driving force for the Finkelstein reaction is the formation of a precipitate, specifically sodium chloride (NaCl), when sodium iodide (NaI) reacts with an alkyl chloride. This precipitation shifts the equilibrium towards the products, facilitating the forward reaction. While NaI is a good nucleophile and the reaction follows an SN2 mechanism, the key factor that drives the reaction forward is the formation of the insoluble product, which removes reactants from the solution and promotes the reaction's progression.

questions.question: Among ${PCl}_{3}, {PCl}_{5}$ and ${SOCl}_{2}, {SOCl}_{2}$ is prefered to prepare ${R}-{Cl}$ because :

questions.explanation: The solution is correct because when preparing alkyl chlorides, using SOCl2 is preferred over PCl3 and PCl5. The reason is that PCl3 and PCl5 produce gaseous byproducts (Cl2 and PCl5), which can escape and make the reaction less efficient. In contrast, SOCl2 does not produce such gases, allowing for a more controlled reaction environment and better yields of the desired product.
